Cross-Entropy Method: Pendulum Swing-Up¶

The Cross-Entropy Method (CEM) is a simple, gradient-free optimization algorithm:

  1. Sample parameter vectors from a Gaussian distribution
  2. Evaluate each by simulating the system
  3. Select the top performers ("elites")
  4. Update the distribution mean and std from the elites

No gradients needed — just simulation + sorting. We use np_cvode as the physics engine and np_randn for sampling.

In [59]:
load("numerics")$
load("numerics-sundials")$
load("numerics-learn")$
load("ax-plots")$

1. Pendulum Dynamics¶

Simple pendulum with torque input $u$: $$\ddot{\theta} = -\frac{g}{l}\sin\theta - b\dot{\theta} + \frac{u}{ml^2}$$

Goal: swing from hanging ($\theta = \pi$) to upright ($\theta = 0$).

In [60]:
/* Pendulum parameters */
gv : 9.81$  lp : 1.0$  mp : 1.0$  bp : 0.1$

/* Max torque (saturation) */
u_max : 5.0$

2. Policy and Cost Function¶

Linear policy in features $[\sin\theta, \cos\theta, \omega]$: $$u = \text{clip}(w_1 \sin\theta + w_2 \cos\theta + w_3 \omega,\; -u_{\max},\; u_{\max})$$

These features are smooth and periodic, unlike raw $\theta$.

In [61]:
/* Cost function: simulate one episode, return scalar cost.
   Lower is better. Penalizes deviation from upright + control effort. */
simulate_cost(w1v, w2v, w3v) := block(
  [u_expr, rhs1, rhs2, tspan_sim, traj, th_col, om_col, cost_state, n_t],
  /* Build closed-loop dynamics with clipped control */
  u_expr : max(-u_max, min(u_max,
    w1v * sin(theta) + w2v * cos(theta) + w3v * omega)),
  rhs1 : omega,
  rhs2 : -(gv/lp)*sin(theta) - bp*omega + u_expr/(mp*lp^2),
  tspan_sim : np_linspace(0, 6, 300),
  /* Start hanging down: theta = pi */
  traj : np_cvode([rhs1, rhs2], [t, theta, omega],
                  [float(%pi), 0.0], tspan_sim, 1e-8, 1e-8),
  /* Cost: (1 - cos(theta))^2 + 0.1*omega^2, weighted toward final states */
  th_col : np_slice(traj, all, 1),
  om_col : np_slice(traj, all, 2),
  cost_state : np_add(
    np_pow(np_sub(1.0, np_cos(th_col)), 2),
    np_scale(0.1, np_pow(om_col, 2))),
  n_t : first(np_shape(traj)),
  float(np_mean(cost_state) + 2.0 * np_ref(cost_state, n_t - 1))
)$

/* Test: cost of zero controller (no torque, hangs at pi forever) */
print("Zero-controller cost:", simulate_cost(0.0, 0.0, 0.0))$

/* Wrapper for np_cem: takes an ndarray parameter vector, returns scalar cost */
cem_cost(w) := simulate_cost(np_ref(w, 0), np_ref(w, 1), np_ref(w, 2))$

Zero-controller cost: 12.0

3. Cross-Entropy Method¶

np_cem implements the CEM optimization loop:

  1. Sample: draw n_samples parameter vectors from $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$
  2. Evaluate: run each through the cost function (here, a full ODE simulation)
  3. Select: sort by cost, keep the best n_elites
  4. Update: set $\mu$ and $\sigma$ to the elite mean and standard deviation

After n_gens generations, the distribution has collapsed around a good solution. Failed cost evaluations (e.g., divergent simulations) are automatically assigned $+\infty$.

In [62]:
np_seed(42)$

/* Run CEM optimization.
   cem_cost(w_ndarray) -> scalar is the only user function.
   np_cem handles sampling, sorting, elite selection, and distribution updates. */
[best_w, cost_history] : np_cem(cem_cost, 3,
  n_samples=40, n_elites=8, n_gens=25, sigma0=5.0, sigma_min=0.3)$

cem_mu : np_to_list(best_w)$
best_cost_history : np_to_list(cost_history)$

print("Final policy: w =",
  map(lambda([x], float(round(x * 100) / 100)), cem_mu))$
print("Final cost:", last(best_cost_history))$

Final policy: w = [-7.2,13.48,-7.1]
Final cost: 0.7464170846123029
In [63]:
/* Learning curve */
gens_list : makelist(i, i, 1, 25)$
ax_draw2d(
  line_width = 2,
  color = royalblue,
  name = "Best cost",
  lines(gens_list, best_cost_history),
  title = "CEM Learning Curve",
  xlabel = "Generation",
  ylabel = "Cost",
  grid = true
)$

4. Evaluate the Learned Policy¶

Simulate the trained controller and compare with the uncontrolled (damped free-swing) pendulum.

In [64]:
/* Simulate trained policy */
w1_opt : cem_mu[1]$  w2_opt : cem_mu[2]$  w3_opt : cem_mu[3]$
u_opt : max(-u_max, min(u_max,
  w1_opt * sin(theta) + w2_opt * cos(theta) + w3_opt * omega))$
rhs1_opt : omega$
rhs2_opt : -(gv/lp)*sin(theta) - bp*omega + u_opt/(mp*lp^2)$

tspan_eval : np_linspace(0, 10, 500)$
traj_opt : np_cvode([rhs1_opt, rhs2_opt], [t, theta, omega],
                     [float(%pi), 0.0], tspan_eval, 1e-8, 1e-8)$

/* Uncontrolled pendulum */
traj_free : np_cvode([omega, -(gv/lp)*sin(theta) - bp*omega],
                      [t, theta, omega],
                      [float(%pi), 0.0], tspan_eval, 1e-8, 1e-8)$

t_eval : np_to_list(np_slice(traj_opt, all, 0))$
th_opt_list : np_to_list(np_slice(traj_opt, all, 1))$
th_free_list : np_to_list(np_slice(traj_free, all, 1))$

/* Convert radians to degrees (unwrapped to avoid discontinuities at ±180°) */
to_deg(a) := a * 180 / float(%pi)$

ax_draw2d(
  line_width = 2,
  color = royalblue,
  name = "CEM policy",
  lines(t_eval, map(to_deg, th_opt_list)),
  color = "#aaa",
  dash = "dash",
  name = "Uncontrolled",
  lines(t_eval, map(to_deg, th_free_list)),
  title = "Pendulum Swing-Up: CEM vs Uncontrolled",
  xlabel = "Time (s)",
  ylabel = "theta (deg)",
  grid = true
)$

rat: replaced 5.0 by 5/1 = 5.0
rat: replaced 7.097577462453554 by 92809385/13076206 = 7.097577462453558
rat: replaced -13.480385615786773 by -28468458/2111843 = -13.48038561578678
rat: replaced 7.202712688558192 by 47069144/6534919 = 7.20271268855819
rat: replaced 5.0 by 5/1 = 5.0

rat: replaced 13.480385615786773 by 28468458/2111843 = 13.48038561578678
rat: replaced -7.202712688558192 by -47069144/6534919 = -7.20271268855819
rat: replaced 5.0 by 5/1 = 5.0
In [65]:
/* Phase portrait */
om_opt_list : np_to_list(np_slice(traj_opt, all, 2))$
ax_draw2d(
  line_width = 2,
  color = royalblue,
  name = "Trajectory",
  lines(map(to_deg, th_opt_list),
        map(lambda([a], a * 180/float(%pi)), om_opt_list)),
  marker_size = 10,
  color = tomato,
  name = "Start (hanging)",
  points([to_deg(float(%pi))], [0]),
  color = forestgreen,
  name = "Target (upright)",
  points([0], [0]),
  title = "Phase Portrait: Swing-Up",
  xlabel = "theta (deg)",
  ylabel = "omega (deg/s)",
  grid = true
)$

Why does the trajectory stall at ~31°?¶

The phase portrait shows the pendulum spiralling toward an equilibrium around $\theta \approx 31°$, not reaching upright ($\theta = 0$). This is a torque saturation effect.

The learned weights are large ($|\mathbf{w}| \approx 15$) but the torque clips at $u_{\max} = 5\,\text{Nm}$, so the controller is pinned at maximum torque for most of the trajectory. The equilibrium is where that constant torque balances gravity:

$$u_{\max} = mgl\sin\theta \quad\Rightarrow\quad \theta^* = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{u_{\max}}{mgl}\right) = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{5}{9.81}\right) \approx 30.6°$$

With $u_{\max} = 5$ and $mgl = 9.81$, the motor simply cannot hold the pendulum upright — it would need $u_{\max} \geq 9.81$ just to balance at the top. The CEM correctly found the best achievable policy: swing as high as the motor allows, then park at the highest sustainable angle.

To achieve a full swing-up we need to increase the torque limit.

5. Increasing the Torque Limit¶

With $u_{\max} = 12\,\text{Nm}$ the motor can now statically hold the pendulum at any angle ($12 > mgl = 9.81$). Re-run CEM with the same policy structure.

In [66]:
/* Increase torque limit and re-run CEM */
u_max : 12.0$
np_seed(42)$

[best_w2, cost_history2] : np_cem(cem_cost, 3,
  n_samples=40, n_elites=8, n_gens=25, sigma0=5.0, sigma_min=0.3)$

cem_mu2 : np_to_list(best_w2)$
print("Higher-torque policy: w =",
  map(lambda([x], float(round(x * 100) / 100)), cem_mu2))$
print("Final cost:", last(np_to_list(cost_history2)))$

In [67]:
/* Simulate higher-torque policy */
w1_2 : cem_mu2[1]$  w2_2 : cem_mu2[2]$  w3_2 : cem_mu2[3]$
u_2 : max(-u_max, min(u_max,
  w1_2 * sin(theta) + w2_2 * cos(theta) + w3_2 * omega))$

traj2 : np_cvode([omega, -(gv/lp)*sin(theta) - bp*omega + u_2/(mp*lp^2)],
                  [t, theta, omega],
                  [float(%pi), 0.0], tspan_eval, 1e-8, 1e-8)$

t2 : np_to_list(np_slice(traj2, all, 0))$
th2 : np_to_list(np_slice(traj2, all, 1))$
om2 : np_to_list(np_slice(traj2, all, 2))$

ax_draw2d(
  line_width = 2,
  color = royalblue,
  name = "u_max = 12",
  lines(t2, map(to_deg, th2)),
  color = "#aaa",
  dash = "dash",
  name = "Uncontrolled",
  lines(t_eval, map(to_deg, th_free_list)),
  title = "Swing-Up with Higher Torque",
  xlabel = "Time (s)",
  ylabel = "theta (deg)",
  grid = true
)$

In [68]:
/* Phase portrait — higher torque */
ax_draw2d(
  line_width = 2,
  color = royalblue,
  name = "Trajectory",
  lines(map(to_deg, th2),
        map(lambda([a], a * 180/float(%pi)), om2)),
  marker_size = 10,
  color = tomato,
  name = "Start (hanging)",
  points([180], [0]),
  color = forestgreen,
  name = "Target (upright)",
  points([0], [0]),
  title = "Phase Portrait: Swing-Up (u_max = 12)",
  xlabel = "theta (deg)",
  ylabel = "omega (deg/s)",
  grid = true
)$

Still not upright — why?¶

The torque limit is no longer the bottleneck ($u_{\max} = 12 > mgl = 9.81$), yet the pendulum settles at $\theta^* \approx 28°$. The problem is the policy structure.

Near upright ($\theta \approx 0$), $\cos\theta \approx 1$, so the policy becomes:

$$u \approx w_1\theta + w_2 + w_3\omega$$

The $w_2$ term is a constant bias that shifts the equilibrium away from $\theta = 0$. Setting $w_2 = 0$ would fix the equilibrium, but then the policy has no mechanism to initiate the swing from hanging ($\theta = \pi$). A linear policy in $[\sin\theta, \cos\theta, \omega]$ cannot express both "push hard when hanging" and "stop pushing at upright."

6. Better Features¶

Replace $\cos\theta$ with $1 - \cos\theta$ in the feature vector:

$$u = \text{clip}(w_1 \sin\theta + w_2 (1 - \cos\theta) + w_3 \omega,\; -u_{\max},\; u_{\max})$$

This feature is 0 at upright and 2 at hanging, so it provides swing-up torque that vanishes at the target. Near upright: $u \approx w_1 \theta + w_2 \theta^2/2 + w_3 \omega$, which has its equilibrium at $\theta = 0$.

In [69]:
/* Cost function with improved features: [sin(theta), 1-cos(theta), omega] */
simulate_cost3(w1v, w2v, w3v) := block(
  [u_expr, rhs1, rhs2, tspan_sim, traj, th_col, om_col, cost_state, n_t],
  u_expr : max(-u_max, min(u_max,
    w1v * sin(theta) + w2v * (1 - cos(theta)) + w3v * omega)),
  rhs1 : omega,
  rhs2 : -(gv/lp)*sin(theta) - bp*omega + u_expr/(mp*lp^2),
  tspan_sim : np_linspace(0, 6, 300),
  traj : np_cvode([rhs1, rhs2], [t, theta, omega],
                  [float(%pi), 0.0], tspan_sim, 1e-8, 1e-8),
  th_col : np_slice(traj, all, 1),
  om_col : np_slice(traj, all, 2),
  cost_state : np_add(
    np_pow(np_sub(1.0, np_cos(th_col)), 2),
    np_scale(0.1, np_pow(om_col, 2))),
  n_t : first(np_shape(traj)),
  float(np_mean(cost_state) + 2.0 * np_ref(cost_state, n_t - 1))
)$

cem_cost3(w) := simulate_cost3(np_ref(w, 0), np_ref(w, 1), np_ref(w, 2))$

np_seed(42)$
[best_w3, cost_history3] : np_cem(cem_cost3, 3,
  n_samples=50, n_elites=10, n_gens=50, sigma0=5.0, sigma_min=0.3)$

cem_mu3 : np_to_list(best_w3)$
print("Improved policy: w =",
  map(lambda([x], float(round(x * 100) / 100)), cem_mu3))$
print("Final cost:", last(np_to_list(cost_history3)))$

rat: replaced 9.342257049812703 by 53203911/5694974 = 9.342257049812694
rat: replaced 12.0 by 12/1 = 12.0
Improved policy: w = [9.32,-25.34,-11.97]
Final cost: 0.45191839552305574
In [70]:
/* Simulate improved policy */
w1_3 : cem_mu3[1]$  w2_3 : cem_mu3[2]$  w3_3 : cem_mu3[3]$
u_3 : max(-u_max, min(u_max,
  w1_3 * sin(theta) + w2_3 * (1 - cos(theta)) + w3_3 * omega))$

traj3 : np_cvode([omega, -(gv/lp)*sin(theta) - bp*omega + u_3/(mp*lp^2)],
                  [t, theta, omega],
                  [float(%pi), 0.0], tspan_eval, 1e-8, 1e-8)$

t3 : np_to_list(np_slice(traj3, all, 0))$
th3 : np_to_list(np_slice(traj3, all, 1))$
om3 : np_to_list(np_slice(traj3, all, 2))$

/* Plot angular error from upright: acos(cos(theta)) is always in [0, pi],
   giving shortest angular distance to the nearest upright position. */
err_deg(a) := acos(cos(a)) * 180 / float(%pi)$

ax_draw2d(
  line_width = 2,
  color = royalblue,
  name = "Improved policy",
  lines(t3, map(err_deg, th3)),
  color = "#aaa",
  dash = "dash",
  name = "Uncontrolled",
  lines(t_eval, map(err_deg, th_free_list)),
  title = "Angular Error from Upright",
  xlabel = "Time (s)",
  ylabel = "Error (deg)",
  grid = true
)$

rat: replaced 12.0 by 12/1 = 12.0
rat: replaced 11.972426532051317 by 208039145/17376523 = 11.97242653205132
rat: replaced 25.335135755624652 by 48114235/1899111 = 25.335135755624606
rat: replaced -9.319166708813365 by -51777225/5555993 = -9.319166708813349
rat: replaced 12.0 by 12/1 = 12.0
rat: replaced -11.972426532051317 by -208039145/17376523 = -11.97242653205132
rat: replaced -25.335135755624652 by -48114235/1899111 = -25.335135755624606
rat: replaced 9.319166708813365 by 51777225/5555993 = 9.319166708813349
rat: replaced 12.0 by 12/1 = 12.0
In [71]:
/* Phase portrait — angular error from upright */
ax_draw2d(
  line_width = 2,
  color = royalblue,
  name = "Trajectory",
  lines(map(err_deg, th3),
        map(lambda([a], a * 180/float(%pi)), om3)),
  marker_size = 10,
  color = tomato,
  name = "Start (hanging)",
  points([180], [0]),
  color = forestgreen,
  name = "Target (upright)",
  points([0], [0]),
  title = "Phase Portrait: Improved Features (u_max = 12)",
  xlabel = "Error from upright (deg)",
  ylabel = "omega (deg/s)",
  grid = true
)$

Key Takeaways¶

  • CEM is simple: no gradients, no backpropagation — just sample, evaluate, select, repeat
  • np_cvode as the environment: each CEM sample runs a full ODE simulation as its "episode"
  • Actuator limits matter: with $u_{\max} = 5$ the motor cannot reach upright — CEM correctly finds the best achievable policy, parking at the torque-limited equilibrium
  • Feature engineering matters: the original $\cos\theta$ feature creates a constant bias that shifts the equilibrium away from upright. Replacing it with $1 - \cos\theta$ — which vanishes at the target — lets the policy converge to $\theta = 0$
  • Maxima's symbolic expressions: the policy and dynamics are symbolic, compiled to efficient native code by np_cvode's expression mode — no callback overhead

For higher-dimensional problems, gradient-based methods (using np_minimize with np_compile_gradient) would be more sample-efficient.
